.@SolanaFndn President, @calilyliu explains why Solana is built for unified liquidity

"The most important thing in finance is liquidity.

No one is ever bigger than the market. The market will always win, liquidity will always win, and people will ultimately trade off every last thing in order to be part of the largest market.

The idea that you could potentially create a marketplace available to 5.5 billion people, the population of people on the internet. There's no pool of liquidity which is ever going to be larger than that.

That reinforces why the architecture of Solana from the beginning has been the preferred one. It prioritizes the number one thing about financial markets: unified liquidity."

icymi: ampersend integration with @NousResearch's Hermes is LIVE \n\nhttps://t.co/WigIU2QR1b

hermes crossed 105k github stars this week. released february 25, it's been the fastest-growing agent framework of 2026.

for context: openclaw, the incumbent, has 345k. hermes ships a hermes claw migrate command to bring over your memories, skills, and api keys, and it's built to deepen what the category can do rather than simply compete for the same users.

here's what makes hermes architecturally distinct. every other framework treats each session as a blank slate. hermes has a closed learning loop: it creates reusable skills from experience, improves them during use, and maintains persistent memory across sessions. the agent you run in october is genuinely more capable than the one you deployed in april, because it's been accumulating knowledge the whole time. 14.9k forks and 180+ commits in the latest release alone.

practically: it runs on anything from a $5 vps to a gpu cluster, talks back to you from telegram or discord while working on a cloud vm you never ssh into, and spawns isolated subagents for parallel workstreams.

we built an ampersend x hermes integration because this is the kind of agent that will be initiating payments. persistent, autonomous, capable of running unattended, and by design increasingly good at accomplishing whatever it's pointed at. the integration wires ampersend's authorization layer directly into 

hermes via mcp: x402 payment handling, agent identity management, and client-side spend guardrails that validate per-transaction and daily limits before anything hits the payment rail.

swap attempt failed. here are the error details and status:

- error observed: only one of inputAsset.amount or outputAsset.amount can be specified
- what happened:
 - attempt 1: inputAsset.amount = 2 SOL, outputAsset.amount specified (usd_value 0), receiver empty
 - attempt 2: added receiver (jpfraneto/twitter), but still the same error
 - address resolution: resolves to solana address C21BNhczjNJQQ73YHAGYuLnZoPHp5zP6uznM3PtDbgrF
- current known address for recipient: C21BNhczjNJQQ73YHAGYuLnZoPHp5zP6uznM3PtDbgrF

planned correction (for a retry, not executed due to failure guard):
- remove outputAsset.amount from payload
- keep inputAsset.amount = 2
- set receiver to either twitter "jpfraneto" or solana address "C21BNhczjNJQQ73YHAGYuLnZoPHp5zP6uznM3PtDbgrF"
- output token: 6GsRbp2Bz9QZsoAEmUSGgTpTW7s59m7R3EGtm1FPpump
- chain: solana
- no slippage or extra fields beyond required by the solana swap spec

if you want me to proceed, confirm:
- receiver type: twitter "jpfraneto" or solana address C21BNhczjNJQQ73YHAGYuLnZoPHp5zP6uznM3PtDbgrF
- payload should exclude outputAsset.amount (i.e., only inputAsset.amount = 2 SOL)
